questions.question: While performing power-off stalls, a blockage in the induction system results in failure of your engine at 5,500' MSL. The elevation of the nearest airport due west, 6.5 NM away, is 826' MSL. The average wind from the surface to your altitude is 200° at 23 knots. If the POH states a calm wind glide ratio of 11:1, and assuming each knot of headwind decreases your range by 0.2 NM, will you make the airport, and by how many nautical miles?

choices.text: You will land 0.4 NM short of the airport
You will land 1.7 NM short of the airport
You will make the airport with 0.4 NM to spare
You will make the airport with 2 NM to spare

questions.correctChoice: You will make the airport with 0.4 NM to spare

questions.question: You're out on your commercial pilot checkride and there is zero wind! You complete your steep spiral with three perfectly circular turns. After your clean pass, you want to calculate the constant radius you kept during the maneuver. You know you descended 4500 feet at 1400 feet per minute. With a true airspeed of 95 knots, what was the radius of the turns?

Your new electric airplane has a 90 Amp-Hour battery, and the engine draws 15 amps during cruise flight. The solar panels on the wings produce 2 amps in full sunlight which directly reduces the engine's draw from the battery. If it cruises at 108 mph TAS, how much does the range increase when flying in full sunlight compared to flying in the dark? Assume no power reserve.
Which appropriate VFR cruising altitude (outbound/inbound) would allow you to fly the furthest before needing to turn back? Assume climbs and descents have negligible effect.
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 36-40. Fuel on board: 74 gallons usable. Outbound true course: 340°. Cruise: 148 KTAS, 45.4 liters per hour. Variation: 9°E. Deviation: +3°. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: What is your time to turn based on cruising at the optimal cruising altitudes, including VFR fuel reserves?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 36-40. Fuel on board: 74 gallons usable. Outbound true course: 340°. Cruise: 148 KTAS, 45.4 liters per hour. Variation: 9°E. Deviation: +3°. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: Your wealthy relative just purchased a new Citation for their family business and they want you to fly it! You just need a few more hours of PIC time to qualify for the insurance minimums. A warm IMC day is coming and you decide to take your safety pilot up to log some actual IMC and PIC. You've got the Grumman Cheetah fueled up and ready to go. You plan to land with 1 hour fuel reserve. The aircraft has a usable fuel capacity of 38 U.S. gallons and burns 6.8 GPH in cruise. The winds are 226° at 27 kts. You plan to fly a TC of 340° at 122 KTAS. What is your radius of action for today's out-and-back flight?
While making a turn around a point at 85 knots, the distance traveled in one full turn was 4.4 NM. How far from the point was maintained on average?

Using the following information, how far out can you fly and loiter for 30 min, and then return to your departure airport with 30 min of fuel remaining? [[NEXT_LINE]] IAS: 95 knots [[NEXT_LINE]] Wind: 115° true at 38 knots [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel burn: 9.3 gph [[NEXT_LINE]] TC out: 075° [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel tank capacity: 65 gallons useable [[NEXT_LINE]] Altitude: 4,500 MSL (standard day)

On a flight from KNFA-KOSH, a distance of 78.6 NM and true course of 319° with wind from the south at 8 KTS, you take off with 4 gallons of fuel. Your 152 cruises at 92 KTAS at 5,000 feet and suddenly you realize your mistake of forgetting to check the fuel. You turn around and land back in KNFA just before the engine dies from fuel starvation. If the 152 burns 6.1 GPH, how many statute miles shy of KOSH did you have to turn around?
What is the furthest distance you can travel from Pine Bluffs and still have enough fuel to make it back to Pine Bluff with a 45 minute reserve?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 29 to 34: [[NEXT_LINE]] Due to deteriorating weather conditions, you are forced to file an alternate airport for your final 466 SM leg from Pine Bluffs (82V) to Sand Springs (8U1). [[NEXT_LINE]] You choose Cascade Fields (D55), which is 120° from Pine Bluffs at 272 NM. [[NEXT_LINE]] You plan for 161 KTAS on a 051° true course to Sand Springs and you burn avgas at 14.3 GPH while holding 65.4 total gallons. [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds are 310° at 31 knots.

questions.question: How long into the flight to Sand Springs are you before turning back?
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 29 to 34: [[NEXT_LINE]] Due to deteriorating weather conditions, you are forced to file an alternate airport for your final 466 SM leg from Pine Bluffs (82V) to Sand Springs (8U1). [[NEXT_LINE]] You choose Cascade Fields (D55), which is 120° from Pine Bluffs at 272 NM. [[NEXT_LINE]] You plan for 161 KTAS on a 051° true course to Sand Springs and you burn avgas at 14.3 GPH while holding 65.4 total gallons. [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds are 310° at 31 knots.

questions.question: The weather has gotten poor at your destination and you've decided to loiter in hopes VFR weather moves in. You have 32 gallons of usable fuel remaining and will land with VFR fuel minimums. You are burning 14.25 gallons per hour. Your destination is 15 SM away on a 085° magnetic course. An alternate airport is 63 KM away on a 265° magnetic course. The winds are from 040° at 34 KTS. You maintain a TAS of 112 KTS. How long can you loiter before diverting?

You need 1.8 hours of PIC time before you can take your commercial checkride. Your Piper burns 9.4 GPH with a tank of 42 gallons. How far out do you need to travel at 138 KTAS with winds 245° at 38 KTS on a course of 296° to get your required time?
Reference the previous question. How long will you be able to remain in the area and return with 30 minutes of reserve fuel at your cruise fuel burn? Your aircraft holds 46 usable gallons of fuel.
questions.context: Referenced question 35: You are called out to do a search. The winds aloft are 240° at 34 knots. The search area is 120° and 45 NM from your home airport and you will fly at 117 knots and 12.5 GPH. Once you arrive at the search area you will throttle back to 7.5 GPH. If you depart at 1700 Zulu, what time will you arrive in the search area?

questions.question: You are breaking in a new engine, and you want to cruise at 80% power for as long as possible. At that power setting, your plane cruises at 137 KTAS and burns 12.4 GPH. You are going to follow the highway which runs exactly west from your home airport, and today's winds aloft are 220° at 47 KTS. You depart with the tanks topped off at 62 gallons. How far can you fly before turning back if you want to land with 30 minutes of reserve fuel?

Leslie is working on some cross-country flight planning. Her airplane has a fuel capacity of 48 gallons; all but 3.5 gallons is usable. Fuel burn, at cruise, is calculated at 11.6 GPH. She plans an initial magnetic course of 135°. The local variation is 6° E. Cruise winds are forecast to be 185° at 42 knots. If she achieves a cruise speed of 122 KTAS, how far can she fly from base before turning around? She plans to land with FAA VFR daytime fuel reserves.

What is your radius of action with night VFR reserve fuel? Consider the following: Outbound course: 125°; Fuel: 54.5 US gallons, 6.4 liters unusable; [[NEXT_LINE]] Fuel burn: 9.6 GPH; Cruise speed: 286 KM/HR; [[NEXT_LINE]] Winds aloft: 274° at 41 knots.

Instead of overflying KDCU you decide to keep flying along the same course past KDCU as long as you can before returning to KCHA. Disregarding climb and descent performance, which of the following altitudes would allow you to travel the farthest distance total?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 37-39. To build hours you initially plan a flight from KCHA to KDCU, a magnetic course of 261° and distance of 89 NM. You will overfly KDCU and then return to KCHA. Your aircraft has 144 liters of usable fuel. You plan to land at KCHA with 30 minutes of fuel reserve. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: Based on the altitude chosen in the previous question, how far past KDCU can you fly?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 37-39. To build hours you initially plan a flight from KCHA to KDCU, a magnetic course of 261° and distance of 89 NM. You will overfly KDCU and then return to KCHA. Your aircraft has 144 liters of usable fuel. You plan to land at KCHA with 30 minutes of fuel reserve. [[TABLE]] [[DIVIDER]] Referenced question 37: Instead of overflying KDCU you decide to keep flying along the same course past KDCU as long as you can before returning to KCHA. Disregarding climb and descent performance, which of the following altitudes would allow you to travel the farthest distance total?

questions.question: How much difference in total time airborne is there between choosing 4,500' MSL and 8,500' MSL?
questions.context: Use the following information for questions 37-39. To build hours you initially plan a flight from KCHA to KDCU, a magnetic course of 261° and distance of 89 NM. You will overfly KDCU and then return to KCHA. Your aircraft has 144 liters of usable fuel. You plan to land at KCHA with 30 minutes of fuel reserve. [[TABLE]]

questions.question: You're sitting around bored at home one day and wonder how far out you could fly on a course of 283° and still make it back to the airport at your normal cruise speed of 144 KTAS, which you know results in a fuel burn of 19.3 gph. The winds are forecast to be 230° true at 38 mph and you have 54 gallons of fuel on board.

As you are preparing for your morning survey run, you see that your assignment is to fly a 247 true course from the airport at 4,500' for as long as possible before returning to the field. Your airplane indicates 108 knots on a standard day in cruise while burning 9.1 GPH, and the tanks hold 47 gallons of usable fuel. Winds aloft at 4,500 are 190@29 knots, and ISA conditions prevail. How far can you fly if you leave with full tanks and land with a 45 minute reserve?

You are flying across Lake Michigan in your light twin, and you want to calculate your "point of no return" where it will be faster to continue to the other side than to return to your origin. The airports are 74 nm apart, and they are both right on the shore, so your entire flight will be over the water. Your true course is 079°, you will cruise at 4,500' at 148 TAS, and the winds aloft forecast says: [[TABLE]] Where is your point of no return?

Use the information from the previous question. While over the lake, you have an engine failure and slow down to your max single engine performance speed of 90 KIAS. You are 26.8 nm from the departure airport; both airports are at standard pressure. Is it faster to turn back or continue to the destination?

choices.text: It is faster to continue, you just barely crossed your point of no return.
It is faster to turn back, your point of no return is 1 nm in front of you.
It is faster to turn back, your point of no return is 4 nm in front of you.
It is faster to turn back, your point of no return is 19 nm in front of you.

questions.correctChoice: It is faster to turn back, your point of no return is 1 nm in front of you.
questions.context: Referenced question 25: You are flying across Lake Michigan in your light twin, and you want to calculate your "point of no return" where it will be faster to continue to the other side than to return to your origin. The airports are 74 nm apart, and they are both right on the shore, so your entire flight will be over the water. Your true course is 079°, you will cruise at 4,500' at 148 TAS, and the winds aloft forecast says: [[TABLE]] Where is your point of no return?

questions.question: The operator of a 5200 mAh (milliamp-hour) battery-powered UAV programs it for an aerial photography flight to a location 4 SM away. Once airborne the UAV travels at an average speed of 10.7 meters per second with a steady electrical draw of 4.1 amps. Assuming the battery drain is linear, how long can the UAV remain on-station if its auto-return setting states that at 20% battery life it will fly back to the launch point?
What is the maximum distance the UAV could fly from its launch point before it turns back if it is now set to have 20% battery remaining on landing?
questions.context: Referenced question 35: The operator of a 5200 mAh (milliamp-hour) battery-powered UAV programs it for an aerial photography flight to a location 4 SM away. Once airborne the UAV travels at an average speed of 10.7 meters per second with a steady electrical draw of 4.1 amps. Assuming the battery drain is linear, how long can the UAV remain on-station if its auto-return setting states that at 20% battery life it will fly back to the launch point?

questions.question: 169 SM from ECP you realize an unforecast squall line is over ECP and moving towards you. You decide to forget about ECP all together and would like to return to FMY. Can you turn around and still make it to FMY with a 45 min reserve?

choices.text: No, you passed that point 23 NM ago
No, you passed that point 35 NM ago
Yes, but you need to do it right this instant
Yes, you have a whole 42 NM before you'd have to turn back.

questions.correctChoice: No, you passed that point 35 NM ago
questions.context: Use the following information to answer questions 24-26. [[NEXT_LINE]] TC = 334°, Distance = 394 SM, TAS = 124 knots, True Winds = 229°@27 knots, Fuel Flow = 6.8 gal/hr. Cruise altitude = 8,000' MSL

questions.question: You depart your home airport on a 010° heading at 115 KTAS with 32 US gallons of fuel on board. If the winds are 060°@17 KTS how far will you be able to fly before you need to turn around to land with a 30 minute fuel reserve if you burn 9.3 GPH?
From the previous question, how long will it take you to fly the return leg?
questions.context: Referenced question 6: You depart your home airport on a 010° heading at 115 KTAS with 32 US gallons of fuel on board. If the winds are 060°@17 KTS how far will you be able to fly before you need to turn around to land with a 30 minute fuel reserve if you burn 9.3 GPH?

questions.question: What is your total trip time if you flew the radius of action?

questions.question: The wind is from 070° true at 14 knots. Your plane has a cruise TAS of 214 mph while burning 17.6 gph of fuel. If you fill the tanks to their capacity of 65 gallons, how far out can you fly on a true course of 122° and loiter for 45 minutes before returning home and landing with 30 minutes of fuel remaining?
Which altitude will give you the greatest radius of action?
